proof_sketch: Shared memory allocation granularity is 128 bytes on modern GPUs. The original array of 256 floats (1024 bytes) and mutated array of 255 floats (1020 bytes) both get padded to 1024 bytes. Therefore, the physical memory layout is identical, and the out-of-bounds access at index 255 still lands within the allocated region, making the mutation a no-op.

Therefore, the physical memory layout is identical, and the out-of-bounds access at index 255 still lands within the allocated region, making the mutation a no-op.",
  "survival_reason": "The mutation changes the declared shared memory size from 256 to 255, but because GPU shared memory is allocated in 128-byte segments, a 255-element float array (1020 bytes) is padded to 1024 bytes, exactly matching the original 256-element allocation. Consequently, the out-of-bounds write and read at index 255 affect the same physical memory as the original code. The kernel's control flow and arithmetic remain unchanged, so all inputs produce bitwise-identical outputs. The extensive testing across value distributions, dtypes, and repeated executions confirmed this equivalence.",
  "killable": false,
  "kill_strategy": "unkillable",
  "suggested_test": null,
  "recommendations": "When declaring shared memory arrays with sizes derived from block dimensions, ensure the size equals the block size exactly and verify alignment assumptions. Use static assertions or dynamic bounds checking in debug mode to catch off-by-one errors; otherwise, testing may mask such mutations due to GPU allocation padding."
}
